revision.comment: Bot: single‑shot reformat + ill→|12=draft + draft categories + iws + DEFAULTSORT + move HTML comments + meta‑cat check
revision.text:
'''Shimotsuki Kagura''' (霜月神楽) refers to {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|12=draft|12=draft}} performances held during the 11th month of the old lunar calendar (霜月), corresponding to {{ill|December|ja|12月|12=draft|12=draft}} through {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft|12=draft}} of the following year in the Gregorian calendar. It is also known as '''{{ill|Yudate Kagura|ja|湯立神楽|12=draft|12=draft}}''' because it is often performed in conjunction with the {{ill|Yudate|ja|湯立|12=draft|12=draft}} ritual. Additionally, as it is believed to be modeled after the kagura of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮外宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, it is sometimes called '''{{ill|Ise-ryū Kagura|ja|伊勢流神楽|12=draft|12=draft}}'''.
== Overview ==
Kagura was traditionally considered an event to be held in the 11th month of the old calendar. In {{ill|ancient|ja|古代|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|medieval Japan|ja|中世|12=draft|12=draft}}, the kagura of the {{ill|Sonkara Shrine|ja|園韓神社|12=draft|12=draft}} at the Imperial Court was performed in November. Additionally, on the 13th day of the 11th lunar month, kagura ceremonies (known as {{ill|Yoriai Kagura|ja|寄合神楽|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hōnō Kagura|ja|奉納神楽|12=draft|12=draft}}) were held at the residence of the {{ill|Hitokuchi Tōdai-fu|ja|一口頭大夫|12=draft|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Goshi of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|御師|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Although the kagura of the Ise Goshi declined during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, it had previously spread across Japan, blending with elements of {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shushō-e|ja|修正会|12=draft|12=draft}}.
In the old calendar, November was considered a time when the gods and nature weakened. Therefore, kagura was performed to revitalize and renew the spirit in preparation for the new year. Rituals from Kumano, such as purification with sacred hot water (湯の清まり) and secluded purification practices (忌籠), were incorporated, becoming the basis for Yudate.
The ritual stage (庭, niwa) is adorned with sacred symbols such as {{ill|Gohei|ja|御幣|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|12=draft|12=draft}}. A hot water cauldron (湯釜, yugama) is placed at the center, with canopies representing clouds and the heavens hanging above. Sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄|12=draft|12=draft}}) and paper decorations ({{ill|Kirigami|ja|切紙飾|12=draft|12=draft}}) encircle the area to welcome the gods. Throughout the night, various prayers, ritual recitations, and kagura dances performed by priests and participants are conducted around the cauldron.
While the specific methods of Shimotsuki Kagura vary by region and shrine, the general ritual sequence includes drawing sacred water from rivers or waterfalls, heating it in cauldrons, offering it to the gods, and sprinkling it on participants for purification ({{ill|Harae|ja|祓|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Misogi|ja|禊|12=draft|12=draft}}). The ceremony begins with the reading of a {{ill|Shinmeichō|ja|神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}} (register of invited deities), followed by:
- Inviting the gods with maskless dances (採物舞, torimono-mai) - Offering praises (願上, negaiage) - Sending off the deities around midnight (神送り, kami-okuri) - A sacred feast (直会, naorai) - Later-night performances featuring masked characters like demons and elders (面形舞, omote-mai) - A final blessing ceremony
Special honors are given to local deities, even if they are of lower rank, due to their deep connection with the community.
== Notable Shimotsuki Kagura Performances ==
[[File:Toyama's Shimotsuki Festival 001.JPG|thumb|Statue from the {{ill|Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|ja|遠山の霜月祭|12=draft|12=draft}}. A hot spring (飲泉) modeled after the Yudate ritual is visible on the right, and the water is drinkable. Photo taken at {{ill|Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|ja|道の駅遠山郷|12=draft|12=draft}} in 2009 ({{ill|Heisei 21|ja|平成21st year|12=draft|12=draft}}).]]
Here are some representative examples of Shimotsuki Kagura. Many other kagura traditions that have preserved parts or all of the Shimotsuki Kagura ritual can be found throughout Japan.
- {{ill|Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|保呂羽山の霜月神楽|12=draft|12=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県横手市大森町八沢木|12=draft|12=draft}}. - {{ill|Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|天龍村の霜月神楽|12=draft|12=draft}}: A designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in {{ill|Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県下伊那郡天龍村|12=draft|12=draft}}. - {{ill|Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|ja|遠山の霜月祭|12=draft|12=draft}}: A festival held in former {{ill|Minami-Shinano Village|ja|南信濃村|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kamimura|ja|上村 (長野県)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県飯田市|12=draft|12=draft}}, designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. - {{ill|Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|ja|花祭 (霜月神楽)|12=draft|12=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in the {{ill|Okumikawa|ja|奥三河|12=draft|12=draft}} region of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== References == * Itaya Toru, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=draft|12=draft}}* 2 ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|12=draft|12=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3. * Kubota Hiromichi, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten* Vol. 1 ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}, 1998), ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Yamaji Kōzō, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihonshi Daijiten* 3 ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft|12=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Aoki Shūhei, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}}* 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986), ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Winter Festivals|ja|冬祭り|12=draft|12=draft}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

[[ja:%E9%9C%9C%E6%9C%88%E7%A5%9E%E6%A5%BD]] [[en:Shimotsuki Kagura]] [[ja:霜月神楽]]

'''Shimotsuki Kagura''' (霜月神楽) refers to {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|12=draft|12=draft}} performances held during the 11th month of the old lunar calendar (霜月), corresponding to {{ill|December|ja|12月|12=draft|12=draft}} through {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft|12=draft}} of the following year in the Gregorian calendar. It is also known as '''{{ill|Yudate Kagura|ja|湯立神楽|12=draft|12=draft}}''' because it is often performed in conjunction with the {{ill|Yudate|ja|湯立|12=draft|12=draft}} ritual. Additionally, as it is believed to be modeled after the kagura of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮外宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, it is sometimes called '''{{ill|Ise-ryū Kagura|ja|伊勢流神楽|12=draft|12=draft}}'''.
== Overview ==
Kagura was traditionally considered an event to be held in the 11th month of the old calendar. In {{ill|ancient|ja|古代|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|medieval Japan|ja|中世|12=draft|12=draft}}, the kagura of the {{ill|Sonkara Shrine|ja|園韓神社|12=draft|12=draft}} at the Imperial Court was performed in November. Additionally, on the 13th day of the 11th lunar month, kagura ceremonies (known as {{ill|Yoriai Kagura|ja|寄合神楽|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hōnō Kagura|ja|奉納神楽|12=draft|12=draft}}) were held at the residence of the {{ill|Hitokuchi Tōdai-fu|ja|一口頭大夫|12=draft|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Goshi of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|御師|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Although the kagura of the Ise Goshi declined during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, it had previously spread across Japan, blending with elements of {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shushō-e|ja|修正会|12=draft|12=draft}}.
In the old calendar, November was considered a time when the gods and nature weakened. Therefore, kagura was performed to revitalize and renew the spirit in preparation for the new year. Rituals from Kumano, such as purification with sacred hot water (湯の清まり) and secluded purification practices (忌籠), were incorporated, becoming the basis for Yudate.
The ritual stage (庭, niwa) is adorned with sacred symbols such as {{ill|Gohei|ja|御幣|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|12=draft|12=draft}}. A hot water cauldron (湯釜, yugama) is placed at the center, with canopies representing clouds and the heavens hanging above. Sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄|12=draft|12=draft}}) and paper decorations ({{ill|Kirigami|ja|切紙飾|12=draft|12=draft}}) encircle the area to welcome the gods. Throughout the night, various prayers, ritual recitations, and kagura dances performed by priests and participants are conducted around the cauldron.
While the specific methods of Shimotsuki Kagura vary by region and shrine, the general ritual sequence includes drawing sacred water from rivers or waterfalls, heating it in cauldrons, offering it to the gods, and sprinkling it on participants for purification ({{ill|Harae|ja|祓|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Misogi|ja|禊|12=draft|12=draft}}). The ceremony begins with the reading of a {{ill|Shinmeichō|ja|神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}} (register of invited deities), followed by:
- Inviting the gods with maskless dances (採物舞, torimono-mai) - Offering praises (願上, negaiage) - Sending off the deities around midnight (神送り, kami-okuri) - A sacred feast (直会, naorai) - Later-night performances featuring masked characters like demons and elders (面形舞, omote-mai) - A final blessing ceremony
Special honors are given to local deities, even if they are of lower Rank, due to their deep connection with the community.
== Notable Shimotsuki Kagura Performances ==
[[File:Toyama's Shimotsuki Festival 001.JPG|thumb|Statue from the {{ill|Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|ja|遠山の霜月祭|12=draft|12=draft}}. A hot spring (飲泉) modeled after the Yudate ritual is visible on the right, and the water is drinkable. Photo taken at {{ill|Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|ja|道の駅遠山郷|12=draft|12=draft}} in 2009 ({{ill|Heisei 21|ja|平成21st year|12=draft|12=draft}}).]]
Here are some representative examples of Shimotsuki Kagura. Many other kagura traditions that have preserved parts or all of the Shimotsuki Kagura ritual can be found throughout Japan.
- {{ill|Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|保呂羽山の霜月神楽|12=draft|12=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県横手市大森町八沢木|12=draft|12=draft}}. - {{ill|Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|天龍村の霜月神楽|12=draft|12=draft}}: A designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in {{ill|Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県下伊那郡天龍村|12=draft|12=draft}}. - {{ill|Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|ja|遠山の霜月祭|12=draft|12=draft}}: A festival held in former {{ill|Minami-Shinano Village|ja|南信濃村|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kamimura|ja|上村 (長野県)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県飯田市|12=draft|12=draft}}, designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. - {{ill|Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|ja|花祭 (霜月神楽)|12=draft|12=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in the {{ill|Okumikawa|ja|奥三河|12=draft|12=draft}} region of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== References == * Itaya Toru, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=draft|12=draft}}* 2 ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|12=draft|12=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3. * Kubota Hiromichi, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten* Vol. 1 ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}, 1998), ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Yamaji Kōzō, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihonshi Daijiten* 3 ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft|12=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Aoki Shūhei, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}}* 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986), ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Winter Festivals|ja|冬祭り|12=draft|12=draft}}

== References == * Itaya Toru, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}* 2 ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3. * Kubota Hiromichi, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten* Vol. 1 ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1998), ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Yamaji Kōzō, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihonshi Daijiten* 3 ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Aoki Shūhei, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}* 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986), ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Winter Festivals|ja|冬祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

revision.text:
'''霜月神楽'''（しもつきかぐら）とは、[[11月_(旧暦)|旧暦11月]]（[[霜月]]、[[グレゴリオ暦]]の[[12月]]から翌年[[1月]]）に行われる[[神楽]]のこと。[[湯立]]と組み合わせて行われていたことから'''[[湯立神楽]]'''（ゆだてかぐら）とも呼ばれ、また[[伊勢神宮]][[外宮]]の神楽を祖型としていることから、'''伊勢流神楽'''（いせりゅうかぐら）とも呼ばれている。 == 概要 == 神楽は本来は旧暦の11月に行われる行事と考えられていたとみられ、[[古代]]・[[中世]]の宮中に鎮座していた[[園韓神社]]の神楽は11月に行われ、また毎年[[11月13日_(旧暦)|旧暦11月13日]]に伊勢神宮外宮の[[御師]]が[[一口頭大夫]]邸にて神楽（寄合神楽・奉納神楽）も11月に行われていた。伊勢御師の神楽は[[明治]]期に衰退したが、それ以前の段階において[[熊野信仰]]や[[修験道]]・[[陰陽道]]・[[修正会]]などの要素と組み合わさった形で日本各地に広まったと考えられている。 旧暦の11月は神や自然が衰弱する時期であり、[[魂振]]の儀式などを行って魂の再生・更新を行って新年に備えるために神楽が行われた。その際に熊野の再生儀式であった湯の清まりと忌籠の呪法が取り入れられてそれが湯立として残ったとされている。 祭場となる舞台の庭には神座として御幣などによる神籬が飾られ、舞台の中央には湯釜が設置され、その天井には白蓋、大乗、雲などの天蓋が下げられ、周囲には注連縄や切紙飾が巡らされて神々を迎える準備がなされる。湯釜を中心として夜を徹して各種の祈祷・祝詞・神人らによる神楽などの舞が行われる。 霜月神楽の方法はそれぞれの地域・神社によって差異はあるものの基本的には儀式に先立って特定の川や滝から汲んだ水を釜で沸かした湯を神前に供えて、祈祷の湯立を行った後に参加者に振り掛けることで[[祓]][[禊]]となし、招待神の名前が書かれた[[神名帳]]を読上げるところから始まり、前夜の素面による採物舞の神招き、神讃めの願上、夜半の招待神の神送り、直会（なおらい）、後夜の鬼や翁などの面形舞の神遊び、祝福の式の順序で行われる。特に神位は低いものの地元とのつながりが密接な神に対しては厚礼をもって接した。 == 代表的な霜月神楽 == [[ファイル:Toyama's Shimotsuki Festival 001.JPG|thumb|遠山の霜月祭の像。右には湯立てを模した[[飲泉]]があり、飲用可。
[[道の駅遠山郷]]にて[[2009年]]（[[平成]]21年）撮影。]] 以下は代表的な霜月神楽を掲げる。この他にも伝統的な霜月神楽の全てあるいは一部を継承した神楽は全国各地に存在している。 * [[秋田県]][[横手市]]大森町八沢木に伝わる[[重要無形民俗文化財]]の民俗芸能。[[保呂羽山の霜月神楽]]を参照。 * [[長野県]][[下伊那郡]][[天龍村]]に伝わる重要無形民俗文化財の民俗芸能。[[天龍村の霜月神楽]]を参照。 * 長野県[[飯田市]]の旧・[[南信濃村]]・[[上村 (長野県)|上村]]に伝わる重要無形民俗文化財の民俗芸能。[[遠山郷#遠山の霜月祭り|遠山の霜月祭]]を参照。 * [[愛知県]]の[[奥三河]]地方に伝わる重要無形民俗文化財の民俗芸能。[[花祭 (霜月神楽)]]を参照。 == 参考文献 == * 板谷徹「霜月神楽」『[[日本歴史大事典]] 2』（[[小学館]] 2000年） ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3 * 久保田裕道「霜月神楽」『日本民俗大辞典 上』（[[吉川弘文館]] 1998年） ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1 * 山路興造「霜月神楽」『日本史大事典 3』（[[平凡社]] 1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1 * 青木周平「霜月神楽」『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] 7』（吉川弘文館 1986年） ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4 == 関連項目 == * [[冬祭り]] {{DEFAULTSORT:しもつきかくら}} [[Category:神楽]] {{Shinto-stub}}

[[道の駅遠山郷]]にて[[2009年]]（[[平成]]21年）撮影。]] 以下は代表的な霜月神楽を掲げる。この他にも伝統的な霜月神楽の全てあるいは一部を継承した神楽は全国各地に存在している。 * [[秋田県]][[横手市]]大森町八沢木に伝わる[[重要無形民俗文化財]]の民俗芸能。[[保呂羽山の霜月神楽]]を参照。 * [[長野県]][[下伊那郡]][[天龍村]]に伝わる重要無形民俗文化財の民俗芸能。[[天龍村の霜月神楽]]を参照。 * 長野県[[飯田市]]の旧・[[南信濃村]]・[[上村 (長野県)|上村]]に伝わる重要無形民俗文化財の民俗芸能。[[遠山郷#遠山の霜月祭り|遠山の霜月祭]]を参照。 * [[愛知県]]の[[奥三河]]地方に伝わる重要無形民俗文化財の民俗芸能。[[花祭 (霜月神楽)]]を参照。 == 参考文献 == * 板谷徹「霜月神楽」『[[日本歴史大事典]] 2』（[[小学館]] 2000年） ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3 * 久保田裕道「霜月神楽」『日本民俗大辞典 上』（[[吉川弘文館]] 1998年） ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1 * 山路興造「霜月神楽」『日本史大事典 3』（[[平凡社]] 1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1 * 青木周平「霜月神楽」『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] 7』（吉川弘文館 1986年） ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4 == 関連項目 == * [[冬祭り]] {{DEFAULTSORT:しもつきかくら}} [[Category:神楽]] {{Shinto-stub}}

'''Shimotsuki Kagura''' (霜月神楽) refers to {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} performances held during the 11th month of the old lunar calendar (霜月), corresponding to {{ill|December|ja|12月|qq=draft|qq=draft}} through {{ill|January|ja|1月|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the following year in the Gregorian calendar. It is also known as '''{{ill|Yudate Kagura|ja|湯立神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' because it is often performed in conjunction with the {{ill|Yudate|ja|湯立|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ritual. Additionally, as it is believed to be modeled after the kagura of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮外宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is sometimes called '''{{ill|Ise-ryū Kagura|ja|伊勢流神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''.
== Overview ==
Kagura was traditionally considered an event to be held in the 11th month of the old calendar. In {{ill|ancient|ja|古代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|medieval Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the kagura of the {{ill|Sonkara Shrine|ja|園韓神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the Imperial Court was performed in November. Additionally, on the 13th day of the 11th lunar month, kagura ceremonies (known as {{ill|Yoriai Kagura|ja|寄合神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hōnō Kagura|ja|奉納神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) were held at the residence of the {{ill|Hitokuchi Tōdai-fu|ja|一口頭大夫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by the {{ill|Goshi of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|御師|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Although the kagura of the Ise Goshi declined during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it had previously spread across Japan, blending with elements of {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shushō-e|ja|修正会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In the old calendar, November was considered a time when the gods and nature weakened. Therefore, kagura was performed to revitalize and renew the spirit in preparation for the new year. Rituals from Kumano, such as purification with sacred hot water (湯の清まり) and secluded purification practices (忌籠), were incorporated, becoming the basis for Yudate.
The ritual stage (庭, niwa) is adorned with sacred symbols such as {{ill|Gohei|ja|御幣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. A hot water cauldron (湯釜, yugama) is placed at the center, with canopies representing clouds and the heavens hanging above. Sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and paper decorations ({{ill|Kirigami|ja|切紙飾|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) encircle the area to welcome the gods. Throughout the night, various prayers, ritual recitations, and kagura dances performed by priests and participants are conducted around the cauldron.
While the specific methods of Shimotsuki Kagura vary by region and shrine, the general ritual sequence includes drawing sacred water from rivers or waterfalls, heating it in cauldrons, offering it to the gods, and sprinkling it on participants for purification ({{ill|Harae|ja|祓|1=Harae|2=ja|3=祓|4=fr|5=Harae|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Misogi|ja|禊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). The ceremony begins with the reading of a {{ill|Shinmeichō|ja|神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (register of invited deities), followed by:
- Inviting the gods with maskless dances (採物舞, torimono-mai) - Offering praises (願上, negaiage) - Sending off the deities around midnight (神送り, kami-okuri) - A sacred feast (直会, naorai) - Later-night performances featuring masked characters like demons and elders (面形舞, omote-mai) - A final blessing ceremony
Special honors are given to local deities, even if they are of lower Rank, due to their deep connection with the community.
== Notable Shimotsuki Kagura Performances ==
[[File:Toyama's Shimotsuki Festival 001.JPG|thumb|Statue from the {{ill|Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. A hot spring (飲泉) modeled after the Yudate ritual is visible on the right, and the water is drinkable. Photo taken at {{ill|Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|ja|道の駅遠山郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 2009 ({{ill|Heisei 21|ja|平成21st year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).]]
Here are some representative examples of Shimotsuki Kagura. Many other kagura traditions that have preserved parts or all of the Shimotsuki Kagura ritual can be found throughout Japan.
- {{ill|Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|保呂羽山の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県横手市大森町八沢木|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. - {{ill|Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|天龍村の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in {{ill|Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県下伊那郡天龍村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. - {{ill|Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A festival held in former {{ill|Minami-Shinano Village|ja|南信濃村|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kamimura|ja|上村 (長野県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県飯田市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. - {{ill|Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|ja|花祭 (霜月神楽)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in the {{ill|Okumikawa|ja|奥三河|qq=draft|qq=draft}} region of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== References == * Itaya Toru, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}* 2 ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3. * Kubota Hiromichi, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten* Vol. 1 ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1998), ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Yamaji Kōzō, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihonshi Daijiten* 3 ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Aoki Shūhei, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}* 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986), ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Winter Festivals|ja|冬祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

In the old calendar, November was considered a time when the gods and nature weakened. Therefore, kagura was performed to revitalize and renew the spirit in preparation for the new year. Rituals from Kumano, such as purification with sacred hot water (湯の清まり) and secluded purification practices (忌籠), were incorporated, becoming the basis for Yudate.
The ritual stage (庭, niwa) is adorned with sacred symbols such as {{ill|Gohei|ja|御幣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. A hot water cauldron (湯釜, yugama) is placed at the center, with canopies representing clouds and the heavens hanging above. Sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and paper decorations ({{ill|Kirigami|ja|切紙飾|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) encircle the area to welcome the gods. Throughout the night, various prayers, ritual recitations, and kagura dances performed by priests and participants are conducted around the cauldron.
While the specific methods of Shimotsuki Kagura vary by region and shrine, the general ritual sequence includes drawing sacred water from rivers or waterfalls, heating it in cauldrons, offering it to the gods, and sprinkling it on participants for purification ({{ill|Harae|ja|祓|1=Harae|2=ja|3=祓|4=fr|5=Harae|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Misogi|ja|禊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). The ceremony begins with the reading of a {{ill|Shinmeichō|ja|神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (register of invited deities), followed by:
- Inviting the gods with maskless dances (採物舞, torimono-mai) - Offering praises (願上, negaiage) - Sending off the deities around midnight (神送り, kami-okuri) - A sacred feast (直会, naorai) - Later-night performances featuring masked characters like demons and elders (面形舞, omote-mai) - A final blessing ceremony
Special honors are given to local deities, even if they are of lower Rank, due to their deep connection with the community.
== Notable Shimotsuki Kagura Performances ==
[[File:Toyama's Shimotsuki Festival 001.JPG|thumb|Statue from the {{ill|Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. A hot spring (飲泉) modeled after the Yudate ritual is visible on the right, and the water is drinkable. Photo taken at {{ill|Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|ja|道の駅遠山郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 2009 ({{ill|Heisei 21|ja|平成21st year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).]]
Here are some representative examples of Shimotsuki Kagura. Many other kagura traditions that have preserved parts or all of the Shimotsuki Kagura ritual can be found throughout Japan.
- {{ill|Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|保呂羽山の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県横手市大森町八沢木|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. - {{ill|Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|天龍村の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in {{ill|Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県下伊那郡天龍村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. - {{ill|Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A festival held in former {{ill|Minami-Shinano Village|ja|南信濃村|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kamimura|ja|上村 (長野県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県飯田市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. - {{ill|Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|ja|花祭 (霜月神楽)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in the {{ill|Okumikawa|ja|奥三河|qq=draft|qq=draft}} region of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== References == * Itaya Toru, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}* 2 ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3. * Kubota Hiromichi, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten* Vol. 1 ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1998), ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Yamaji Kōzō, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihonshi Daijiten* 3 ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Aoki Shūhei, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}* 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986), ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Winter Festivals|ja|冬祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

'''Shimotsuki Kagura''' (霜月神楽) refers to {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} performances held during the 11th month of the old lunar calendar (霜月), corresponding to {{ill|December|ja|12月|qq=draft|qq=draft}} through {{ill|January|ja|1月|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the following year in the Gregorian calendar. It is also known as '''{{ill|Yudate Kagura|ja|湯立神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' because it is often performed in conjunction with the {{ill|Yudate|ja|湯立|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ritual. Additionally, as it is believed to be modeled after the kagura of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮外宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is sometimes called '''{{ill|Ise-ryū Kagura|ja|伊勢流神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''.
== Overview ==
Kagura was traditionally considered an event to be held in the 11th month of the old calendar. In {{ill|ancient|ja|古代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|medieval Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the kagura of the {{ill|Sonkara Shrine|ja|園韓神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the Imperial Court was performed in November. Additionally, on the 13th day of the 11th lunar month, kagura ceremonies (known as {{ill|Yoriai Kagura|ja|寄合神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hōnō Kagura|ja|奉納神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) were held at the residence of the {{ill|Hitokuchi Tōdai-fu|ja|一口頭大夫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by the {{ill|Goshi of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|御師|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Although the kagura of the Ise Goshi declined during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it had previously spread across Japan, blending with elements of {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shushō-e|ja|修正会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In the old calendar, November was considered a time when the gods and nature weakened. Therefore, kagura was performed to revitalize and renew the spirit in preparation for the new year. Rituals from Kumano, such as purification with sacred hot water (湯の清まり) and secluded purification practices (忌籠), were incorporated, becoming the basis for Yudate.
The ritual stage (庭, niwa) is adorned with sacred symbols such as {{ill|Gohei|ja|御幣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Throughout the night, various prayers, ritual recitations, and kagura dances performed by priests and participants are conducted around the cauldron.
While the specific methods of Shimotsuki Kagura vary by region and shrine, the general ritual sequence includes drawing sacred water from rivers or waterfalls, heating it in cauldrons, offering it to the gods, and sprinkling it on participants for purification ({{ill|Harae|ja|祓|1=Harae|2=ja|3=祓|4=fr|5=Harae|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Misogi|ja|禊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). The ceremony begins with the reading of a {{ill|Shinmeichō|ja|神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (register of invited deities), followed by:
- Inviting the gods with maskless dances (採物舞, torimono-mai) - Offering praises (願上, negaiage) - Sending off the deities around midnight (神送り, kami-okuri) - A sacred feast (直会, naorai) - Later-night performances featuring masked characters like demons and elders (面形舞, omote-mai) - A final blessing ceremony
Special honors are given to local deities, even if they are of lower Rank, due to their deep connection with the community.
== Notable Shimotsuki Kagura Performances ==
[[File:Toyama's Shimotsuki Festival 001.JPG|thumb|Statue from the {{ill|Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. A hot spring (飲泉) modeled after the Yudate ritual is visible on the right, and the water is drinkable. Photo taken at {{ill|Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|ja|道の駅遠山郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 2009 ({{ill|Heisei 21|ja|平成21st year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).]]
Here are some representative examples of Shimotsuki Kagura. Many other kagura traditions that have preserved parts or all of the Shimotsuki Kagura ritual can be found throughout Japan.
- {{ill|Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|保呂羽山の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県横手市大森町八沢木|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. - {{ill|Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|天龍村の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in {{ill|Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県下伊那郡天龍村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. - {{ill|Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A festival held in former {{ill|Minami-Shinano Village|ja|南信濃村|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kamimura|ja|上村 (長野県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県飯田市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. - {{ill|Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|ja|花祭 (霜月神楽)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in the {{ill|Okumikawa|ja|奥三河|qq=draft|qq=draft}} region of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.

While the specific methods of Shimotsuki Kagura vary by region and shrine, the general ritual sequence includes drawing sacred water from rivers or waterfalls, heating it in cauldrons, offering it to the gods, and sprinkling it on participants for purification ({{ill|Harae|ja|祓|1=Harae|2=ja|3=祓|4=fr|5=Harae|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Misogi|ja|禊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). The ceremony begins with the reading of a {{ill|Shinmeichō|ja|神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (register of invited deities), followed by:
- Inviting the gods with maskless dances (採物舞, torimono-mai) - Offering praises (願上, negaiage) - Sending off the deities around midnight (神送り, kami-okuri) - A sacred feast (直会, naorai) - Later-night performances featuring masked characters like demons and elders (面形舞, omote-mai) - A final blessing ceremony
Special honors are given to local deities, even if they are of lower Rank, due to their deep connection with the community.
== Notable Shimotsuki Kagura Performances ==
[[File:Toyama's Shimotsuki Festival 001.JPG|thumb|Statue from the {{ill|Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. A hot spring (飲泉) modeled after the Yudate ritual is visible on the right, and the water is drinkable. Photo taken at {{ill|Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|ja|道の駅遠山郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 2009 ({{ill|Heisei 21|ja|平成21st year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).]]
Here are some representative examples of Shimotsuki Kagura. Many other kagura traditions that have preserved parts or all of the Shimotsuki Kagura ritual can be found throughout Japan.
- {{ill|Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|保呂羽山の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県横手市大森町八沢木|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. - {{ill|Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|天龍村の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in {{ill|Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県下伊那郡天龍村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. - {{ill|Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A festival held in former {{ill|Minami-Shinano Village|ja|南信濃村|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kamimura|ja|上村 (長野県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県飯田市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. - {{ill|Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|ja|花祭 (霜月神楽)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in the {{ill|Okumikawa|ja|奥三河|qq=draft|qq=draft}} region of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== References == * Itaya Toru, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}* 2 ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3. * Kubota Hiromichi, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten* Vol. 1 ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1998), ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Yamaji Kōzō, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihonshi Daijiten* 3 ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Aoki Shūhei, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}* 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986), ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Winter Festivals|ja|冬祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

[[sn:Shimotsuki Kagura]] [[ja:%E9%9C%9C%E6%9C%88%E7%A5%9E%E6%A5%BD]] [[en:Shimotsuki Kagura]] [[ja:霜月神楽]]

'''Shimotsuki Kagura''' (霜月神楽) refers to {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} performances held during the 11th month of the old lunar calendar (霜月), corresponding to {{ill|December|ja|12月|qq=draft|qq=draft}} through {{ill|January|ja|1月|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the following year in the Gregorian calendar. It is also known as '''{{ill|Yudate Kagura|ja|湯立神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' because it is often performed in conjunction with the {{ill|Yudate|ja|湯立|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ritual. Additionally, as it is believed to be modeled after the kagura of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮外宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is sometimes called '''{{ill|Ise-ryū Kagura|ja|伊勢流神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''.
== Overview ==
Kagura was traditionally considered an event to be held in the 11th month of the old calendar. In {{ill|ancient|ja|古代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|medieval Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the kagura of the {{ill|Sonkara Shrine|ja|園韓神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the Imperial Court was performed in November. Additionally, on the 13th day of the 11th lunar month, kagura ceremonies (known as {{ill|Yoriai Kagura|ja|寄合神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hōnō Kagura|ja|奉納神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) were held at the residence of the {{ill|Hitokuchi Tōdai-fu|ja|一口頭大夫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by the {{ill|Goshi of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|御師|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Although the kagura of the Ise Goshi declined during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it had previously spread across Japan, blending with elements of {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shushō-e|ja|修正会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In the old calendar, November was considered a time when the gods and nature weakened. Therefore, kagura was performed to revitalize and renew the spirit in preparation for the new year. Rituals from Kumano, such as purification with sacred hot water (湯の清まり) and secluded purification practices (忌籠), were incorporated, becoming the basis for Yudate.
The ritual stage (庭, niwa) is adorned with sacred symbols such as {{ill|Gohei|ja|御幣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. A hot water cauldron (湯釜, yugama) is placed at the center, with canopies representing clouds and the heavens hanging above. Sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and paper decorations ({{ill|Kirigami|ja|切紙飾|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) encircle the area to welcome the gods. Throughout the night, various prayers, ritual recitations, and kagura dances performed by priests and participants are conducted around the cauldron.
While the specific methods of Shimotsuki Kagura vary by region and shrine, the general ritual sequence includes drawing sacred water from rivers or waterfalls, heating it in cauldrons, offering it to the gods, and sprinkling it on participants for purification ({{ill|Harae|ja|祓|1=Harae|2=ja|3=祓|4=fr|5=Harae|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Misogi|ja|禊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). The ceremony begins with the reading of a {{ill|Shinmeichō|ja|神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (register of invited deities), followed by:
- Inviting the gods with maskless dances (採物舞, torimono-mai) - Offering praises (願上, negaiage) - Sending off the deities around midnight (神送り, kami-okuri) - A sacred feast (直会, naorai) - Later-night performances featuring masked characters like demons and elders (面形舞, omote-mai) - A final blessing ceremony
Special honors are given to local deities, even if they are of lower Rank, due to their deep connection with the community.
== Notable Shimotsuki Kagura Performances ==
[[File:Toyama's Shimotsuki Festival 001.JPG|thumb|Statue from the {{ill|Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. A hot spring (飲泉) modeled after the Yudate ritual is visible on the right, and the water is drinkable. Photo taken at {{ill|Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|ja|道の駅遠山郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 2009 ({{ill|Heisei 21|ja|平成21st year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).]]
Here are some representative examples of Shimotsuki Kagura. Many other kagura traditions that have preserved parts or all of the Shimotsuki Kagura ritual can be found throughout Japan.
- {{ill|Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|保呂羽山の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県横手市大森町八沢木|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. - {{ill|Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|天龍村の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in {{ill|Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県下伊那郡天龍村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. - {{ill|Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A festival held in former {{ill|Minami-Shinano Village|ja|南信濃村|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kamimura|ja|上村 (長野県)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県飯田市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. - {{ill|Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|ja|花祭 (霜月神楽)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in the {{ill|Okumikawa|ja|奥三河|qq=draft|qq=draft}} region of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== References == * Itaya Toru, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}* 2 ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3. * Kubota Hiromichi, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten* Vol. 1 ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1998), ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Yamaji Kōzō, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihonshi Daijiten* 3 ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Aoki Shūhei, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}* 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986), ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Winter Festivals|ja|冬祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

== Overview ==
Kagura was traditionally considered an event to be held in the 11th month of the old calendar. In {{ill|ancient|ja|古代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient|qq=}} and {{ill|medieval Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval Japan|qq=}}, the kagura of the {{ill|Sonkara Shrine|ja|園韓神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonkara Shrine|qq=}} at the Imperial Court was performed in November. Additionally, on the 13th day of the 11th lunar month, kagura ceremonies (known as {{ill|Yoriai Kagura|ja|寄合神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoriai Kagura|qq=}} and {{ill|Hōnō Kagura|ja|奉納神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōnō Kagura|qq=}}) were held at the residence of the {{ill|Hitokuchi Tōdai-fu|ja|一口頭大夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitokuchi Tōdai-fu|qq=}} by the {{ill|Goshi of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|御師|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshi of Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}.
Although the kagura of the Ise Goshi declined during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, it had previously spread across Japan, blending with elements of {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}, {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|qq=}}, {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}}, and {{ill|Shushō-e|ja|修正会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shushō-e|qq=}}.
In the old calendar, November was considered a time when the gods and nature weakened. Therefore, kagura was performed to revitalize and renew the spirit in preparation for the new year. Rituals from Kumano, such as purification with sacred hot water (湯の清まり) and secluded purification practices (忌籠), were incorporated, becoming the basis for Yudate.
The ritual stage (庭, niwa) is adorned with sacred symbols such as {{ill|Gohei|ja|御幣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gohei|qq=}} and {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}}. A hot water cauldron (湯釜, yugama) is placed at the center, with canopies representing clouds and the heavens hanging above. Sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimenawa|qq=}}) and paper decorations ({{ill|Kirigami|ja|切紙飾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirigami|qq=}}) encircle the area to welcome the gods. Throughout the night, various prayers, ritual recitations, and kagura dances performed by priests and participants are conducted around the cauldron.
While the specific methods of Shimotsuki Kagura vary by region and shrine, the general ritual sequence includes drawing sacred water from rivers or waterfalls, heating it in cauldrons, offering it to the gods, and sprinkling it on participants for purification ({{ill|Harae|ja|祓|1=Harae|2=ja|3=祓|4=fr|5=Harae|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harae|13=User:Immanuelle/Harae|qq=}} and {{ill|Misogi|ja|禊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misogi|qq=}}). The ceremony begins with the reading of a {{ill|Shinmeichō|ja|神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmeichō|qq=}} (register of invited deities), followed by:
- Inviting the gods with maskless dances (採物舞, torimono-mai) - Offering praises (願上, negaiage) - Sending off the deities around midnight (神送り, kami-okuri) - A sacred feast (直会, naorai) - Later-night performances featuring masked characters like demons and elders (面形舞, omote-mai) - A final blessing ceremony
Special honors are given to local deities, even if they are of lower Rank, due to their deep connection with the community.
== Notable Shimotsuki Kagura Performances ==
[[File:Toyama's Shimotsuki Festival 001.JPG|thumb|Statue from the {{ill|Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|qq=}}. A hot spring (飲泉) modeled after the Yudate ritual is visible on the right, and the water is drinkable. Photo taken at {{ill|Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|ja|道の駅遠山郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|qq=}} in 2009 ({{ill|Heisei 21|ja|平成21st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 21|qq=}}).]]
Here are some representative examples of Shimotsuki Kagura. Many other kagura traditions that have preserved parts or all of the Shimotsuki Kagura ritual can be found throughout Japan.
- {{ill|Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|保呂羽山の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} in {{ill|Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県横手市大森町八沢木|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|qq=}}. - {{ill|Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|天龍村の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}: A designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in {{ill|Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県下伊那郡天龍村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}. - {{ill|Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|qq=}}: A festival held in former {{ill|Minami-Shinano Village|ja|南信濃村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Shinano Village|qq=}} and {{ill|Kamimura|ja|上村 (長野県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimura|qq=}} in {{ill|Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県飯田市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. - {{ill|Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|ja|花祭 (霜月神楽)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|qq=}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in the {{ill|Okumikawa|ja|奥三河|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okumikawa|qq=}} region of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefecture|qq=}}.
== References == * Itaya Toru, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|qq=}}* 2 ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogakukan|qq=}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3. * Kubota Hiromichi, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten* Vol. 1 ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}, 1998), ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Yamaji Kōzō, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihonshi Daijiten* 3 ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha|qq=}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Aoki Shūhei, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}* 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986), ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Winter Festivals|ja|冬祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}}

[[sn:Shimotsuki Kagura]] [[ja:%E9%9C%9C%E6%9C%88%E7%A5%9E%E6%A5%BD]] [[en:Shimotsuki Kagura]] [[ja:霜月神楽]]

'''Shimotsuki Kagura''' (霜月神楽) refers to {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagura|qq=}} performances held during the 11th month of the old lunar calendar (霜月), corresponding to {{ill|December|ja|12月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} through {{ill|January|ja|1月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January|qq=}} of the following year in the Gregorian calendar. It is also known as '''{{ill|Yudate Kagura|ja|湯立神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudate Kagura|qq=}}''' because it is often performed in conjunction with the {{ill|Yudate|ja|湯立|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudate|qq=}} ritual. Additionally, as it is believed to be modeled after the kagura of the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮外宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, it is sometimes called '''{{ill|Ise-ryū Kagura|ja|伊勢流神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-ryū Kagura|qq=}}'''.

- Inviting the gods with maskless dances (採物舞, torimono-mai) - Offering praises (願上, negaiage) - Sending off the deities around midnight (神送り, kami-okuri) - A sacred feast (直会, naorai) - Later-night performances featuring masked characters like demons and elders (面形舞, omote-mai) - A final blessing ceremony

The ritual stage (庭, niwa) is adorned with sacred symbols such as {{ill|Gohei|ja|御幣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gohei|qq=}} and {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}}. A hot water cauldron (湯釜, yugama) is placed at the center, with canopies representing clouds and the heavens hanging above. Sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimenawa|qq=}}) and paper decorations ({{ill|Kirigami|ja|切紙飾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirigami|qq=}}) encircle the area to welcome the gods. Throughout the night, various prayers, ritual recitations, and kagura dances performed by priests and participants are conducted around the cauldron.
While the specific methods of Shimotsuki Kagura vary by region and shrine, the general ritual sequence includes drawing sacred water from rivers or waterfalls, heating it in cauldrons, offering it to the gods, and sprinkling it on participants for purification ({{ill|Harae|ja|祓|1=Harae|2=ja|3=祓|4=fr|5=Harae|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harae|13=User:Immanuelle/Harae|qq=}} and {{ill|Misogi|ja|禊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misogi|qq=}}). The ceremony begins with the reading of a {{ill|Shinmeichō|ja|神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmeichō|qq=}} (register of invited deities), followed by:
- Inviting the gods with maskless dances (採物舞, torimono-mai) - Offering praises (願上, negaiage) - Sending off the deities around midnight (神送り, kami-okuri) - A sacred feast (直会, naorai) - Later-night performances featuring masked characters like demons and elders (面形舞, omote-mai) - A final blessing ceremony
Special honors are given to local deities, even if they are of lower Rank, due to their deep connection with the community.
== Notable Shimotsuki Kagura Performances ==
[[File:Toyama's Shimotsuki Festival 001.JPG|thumb|Statue from the {{ill|Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|qq=}}. A hot spring (飲泉) modeled after the Yudate ritual is visible on the right, and the water is drinkable. Photo taken at {{ill|Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|ja|道の駅遠山郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|qq=}} in 2009 ({{ill|Heisei 21|ja|平成21st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 21|qq=}}).]]
Here are some representative examples of Shimotsuki Kagura. Many other kagura traditions that have preserved parts or all of the Shimotsuki Kagura ritual can be found throughout Japan.
- {{ill|Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|保呂羽山の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} in {{ill|Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県横手市大森町八沢木|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|qq=}}. - {{ill|Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|天龍村の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}: A designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in {{ill|Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県下伊那郡天龍村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}. - {{ill|Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|qq=}}: A festival held in former {{ill|Minami-Shinano Village|ja|南信濃村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Shinano Village|qq=}} and {{ill|Kamimura|ja|上村 (長野県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimura|qq=}} in {{ill|Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県飯田市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. - {{ill|Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|ja|花祭 (霜月神楽)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|qq=}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in the {{ill|Okumikawa|ja|奥三河|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okumikawa|qq=}} region of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefecture|qq=}}.
== References == * Itaya Toru, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|qq=}}* 2 ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogakukan|qq=}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3. * Kubota Hiromichi, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten* Vol. 1 ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}, 1998), ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Yamaji Kōzō, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihonshi Daijiten* 3 ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha|qq=}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Aoki Shūhei, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}* 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986), ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|ja|冬祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}}

[[sn:Shimotsuki Kagura]] [[ja:%E9%9C%9C%E6%9C%88%E7%A5%9E%E6%A5%BD]] [[en:Shimotsuki Kagura]] [[ja:霜月神楽]]

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine]] → [[Kōtai Jingū]]
revision.text:
'''Shimotsuki Kagura''' (霜月神楽) refers to {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagura|qq=}} performances held during the 11th month of the old lunar calendar (霜月), corresponding to {{ill|December|ja|12月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} through {{ill|January|ja|1月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January|qq=}} of the following year in the Gregorian calendar. It is also known as '''{{ill|Yudate Kagura|ja|湯立神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudate Kagura|qq=}}''' because it is often performed in conjunction with the {{ill|Yudate|ja|湯立|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudate|qq=}} ritual. Additionally, as it is believed to be modeled after the kagura of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮外宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, it is sometimes called '''{{ill|Ise-ryū Kagura|ja|伊勢流神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-ryū Kagura|qq=}}'''.
== Overview ==
Kagura was traditionally considered an event to be held in the 11th month of the old calendar. In {{ill|ancient|ja|古代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient|qq=}} and {{ill|medieval Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval Japan|qq=}}, the kagura of the {{ill|Sonkara Shrine|ja|園韓神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonkara Shrine|qq=}} at the Imperial Court was performed in November. Additionally, on the 13th day of the 11th lunar month, kagura ceremonies (known as {{ill|Yoriai Kagura|ja|寄合神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoriai Kagura|qq=}} and {{ill|Hōnō Kagura|ja|奉納神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōnō Kagura|qq=}}) were held at the residence of the {{ill|Hitokuchi Tōdai-fu|ja|一口頭大夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitokuchi Tōdai-fu|qq=}} by the {{ill|Goshi of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|御師|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshi of Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}.
Although the kagura of the Ise Goshi declined during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, it had previously spread across Japan, blending with elements of {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}, {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|qq=}}, {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}}, and {{ill|Shushō-e|ja|修正会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shushō-e|qq=}}.
In the old calendar, November was considered a time when the gods and nature weakened. Therefore, kagura was performed to revitalize and renew the spirit in preparation for the new year. Rituals from Kumano, such as purification with sacred hot water (湯の清まり) and secluded purification practices (忌籠), were incorporated, becoming the basis for Yudate.
The ritual stage (庭, niwa) is adorned with sacred symbols such as {{ill|Gohei|ja|御幣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gohei|qq=}} and {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}}. A hot water cauldron (湯釜, yugama) is placed at the center, with canopies representing clouds and the heavens hanging above. Sacred ropes ({{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimenawa|qq=}}) and paper decorations ({{ill|Kirigami|ja|切紙飾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirigami|qq=}}) encircle the area to welcome the gods. Throughout the night, various prayers, ritual recitations, and kagura dances performed by priests and participants are conducted around the cauldron.
While the specific methods of Shimotsuki Kagura vary by region and shrine, the general ritual sequence includes drawing sacred water from rivers or waterfalls, heating it in cauldrons, offering it to the gods, and sprinkling it on participants for purification ({{ill|Harae|ja|祓|1=Harae|2=ja|3=祓|4=fr|5=Harae|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harae|13=User:Immanuelle/Harae|qq=}} and {{ill|Misogi|ja|禊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misogi|qq=}}). The ceremony begins with the reading of a {{ill|Shinmeichō|ja|神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmeichō|qq=}} (register of invited deities), followed by:
- Inviting the gods with maskless dances (採物舞, torimono-mai) - Offering praises (願上, negaiage) - Sending off the deities around midnight (神送り, kami-okuri) - A sacred feast (直会, naorai) - Later-night performances featuring masked characters like demons and elders (面形舞, omote-mai) - A final blessing ceremony
Special honors are given to local deities, even if they are of lower Rank, due to their deep connection with the community.
== Notable Shimotsuki Kagura Performances ==
[[File:Toyama's Shimotsuki Festival 001.JPG|thumb|Statue from the {{ill|Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|qq=}}. A hot spring (飲泉) modeled after the Yudate ritual is visible on the right, and the water is drinkable. Photo taken at {{ill|Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|ja|道の駅遠山郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|qq=}} in 2009 ({{ill|Heisei 21|ja|平成21st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 21|qq=}}).]]
Here are some representative examples of Shimotsuki Kagura. Many other kagura traditions that have preserved parts or all of the Shimotsuki Kagura ritual can be found throughout Japan.
- {{ill|Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|保呂羽山の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} in {{ill|Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県横手市大森町八沢木|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|qq=}}. - {{ill|Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|天龍村の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}: A designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in {{ill|Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県下伊那郡天龍村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}. - {{ill|Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|qq=}}: A festival held in former {{ill|Minami-Shinano Village|ja|南信濃村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Shinano Village|qq=}} and {{ill|Kamimura|ja|上村 (長野県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimura|qq=}} in {{ill|Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県飯田市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. - {{ill|Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|ja|花祭 (霜月神楽)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|qq=}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in the {{ill|Okumikawa|ja|奥三河|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okumikawa|qq=}} region of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefecture|qq=}}.
== References == * Itaya Toru, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|qq=}}* 2 ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogakukan|qq=}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3. * Kubota Hiromichi, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten* Vol. 1 ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}, 1998), ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Yamaji Kōzō, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihonshi Daijiten* 3 ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha|qq=}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Aoki Shūhei, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}* 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986), ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|ja|冬祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}}

[[File:Toyama's Shimotsuki Festival 001.JPG|thumb|Statue from the {{ill|Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|qq=}}. A hot spring (飲泉) modeled after the Yudate ritual is visible on the right, and the water is drinkable. Photo taken at {{ill|Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|ja|道の駅遠山郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|qq=}} in 2009 ({{ill|Heisei 21|ja|平成21年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 21|qq=}}).]]
Here are some representative examples of Shimotsuki Kagura. Many other kagura traditions that have preserved parts or all of the Shimotsuki Kagura ritual can be found throughout Japan.
- {{ill|Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|保呂羽山の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} in {{ill|Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県横手市大森町八沢木|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|qq=}}. - {{ill|Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|天龍村の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}: A designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in {{ill|Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県下伊那郡天龍村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}. - {{ill|Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|qq=}}: A festival held in former {{ill|Minami-Shinano Village|ja|南信濃村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Shinano Village|qq=}} and {{ill|Kamimura|ja|上村 (長野県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimura|qq=}} in {{ill|Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県飯田市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. - {{ill|Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|ja|花祭 (霜月神楽)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|qq=}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in the {{ill|Okumikawa|ja|奥三河|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okumikawa|qq=}} region of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefecture|qq=}}.

[[sn:Shimotsuki Kagura]] [[ja:%E9%9C%9C%E6%9C%88%E7%A5%9E%E6%A5%BD]] [[en:Shimotsuki Kagura]] [[ja:霜月神楽]]

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
'''Shimotsuki Kagura''' (霜月神楽) refers to [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]] performances held during the 11th month of the old lunar calendar (霜月), corresponding to [[:en:December|December]] through [[:en:January|January]] of the following year in the Gregorian calendar. It is also known as '''{{ill|Yudate Kagura|ja|湯立神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudate Kagura|qq=}}''' because it is often performed in conjunction with the {{ill|Yudate|ja|湯立|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudate|qq=}} ritual. Additionally, as it is believed to be modeled after the kagura of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮外宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, it is sometimes called '''{{ill|Ise-ryū Kagura|ja|伊勢流神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-ryū Kagura|qq=}}'''.
== Overview ==
Kagura was traditionally considered an event to be held in the 11th month of the old calendar. In [[:en:Ancient history|ancient]] and [[:en:Middle Ages|medieval Japan]], the kagura of the {{ill|Sonkara Shrine|ja|園韓神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonkara Shrine|qq=}} at the Imperial Court was performed in November. Additionally, on the 13th day of the 11th lunar month, kagura ceremonies (known as {{ill|Yoriai Kagura|ja|寄合神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoriai Kagura|qq=}} and {{ill|Hōnō Kagura|ja|奉納神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōnō Kagura|qq=}}) were held at the residence of the {{ill|Hitokuchi Tōdai-fu|ja|一口頭大夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitokuchi Tōdai-fu|qq=}} by the {{ill|Goshi of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|御師|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshi of Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}.
Although the kagura of the Ise Goshi declined during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], it had previously spread across Japan, blending with elements of {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}, [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], and {{ill|Shushō-e|ja|修正会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shushō-e|qq=}}.
In the old calendar, November was considered a time when the gods and nature weakened. Therefore, kagura was performed to revitalize and renew the spirit in preparation for the new year. Rituals from Kumano, such as purification with sacred hot water (湯の清まり) and secluded purification practices (忌籠), were incorporated, becoming the basis for Yudate.
The ritual stage (庭, niwa) is adorned with sacred symbols such as [[:en:Gohei|Gohei]] and [[:en:Himorogi|Himorogi]]. A hot water cauldron (湯釜, yugama) is placed at the center, with canopies representing clouds and the heavens hanging above. Sacred ropes ([[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]) and paper decorations ({{ill|Kirigami|ja|切紙飾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirigami|qq=}}) encircle the area to welcome the gods. Throughout the night, various prayers, ritual recitations, and kagura dances performed by priests and participants are conducted around the cauldron.
While the specific methods of Shimotsuki Kagura vary by region and shrine, the general ritual sequence includes drawing sacred water from rivers or waterfalls, heating it in cauldrons, offering it to the gods, and sprinkling it on participants for purification ([[:en:Harae|Harae]] and [[:en:Misogi|Misogi]]). The ceremony begins with the reading of a {{ill|Shinmeichō|ja|神名帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmeichō|qq=}} (register of invited deities), followed by:
- Inviting the gods with maskless dances (採物舞, torimono-mai) - Offering praises (願上, negaiage) - Sending off the deities around midnight (神送り, kami-okuri) - A sacred feast (直会, naorai) - Later-night performances featuring masked characters like demons and elders (面形舞, omote-mai) - A final blessing ceremony
Special honors are given to local deities, even if they are of lower Rank, due to their deep connection with the community.
== Notable Shimotsuki Kagura Performances ==
[[File:Toyama's Shimotsuki Festival 001.JPG|thumb|Statue from the {{ill|Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Shimotsuki Festival|qq=}}. A hot spring (飲泉) modeled after the Yudate ritual is visible on the right, and the water is drinkable. Photo taken at {{ill|Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|ja|道の駅遠山郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi-no-Eki Toyama-go|qq=}} in 2009 ({{ill|Heisei 21|ja|平成21年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei 21|qq=}}).]]
Here are some representative examples of Shimotsuki Kagura. Many other kagura traditions that have preserved parts or all of the Shimotsuki Kagura ritual can be found throughout Japan.
- {{ill|Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|保呂羽山の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horowasan no Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} in {{ill|Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県横手市大森町八沢木|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori, Yokote City, Akita Prefecture|qq=}}. - {{ill|Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|天龍村の霜月神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-mura no Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}: A designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in {{ill|Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県下伊那郡天龍村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū Village, Shimoina District, Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}. - {{ill|Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|ja|遠山の霜月祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōyama no Shimotsuki Matsuri|qq=}}: A festival held in former [[:en:Minamishinano, Nagano|Minami-Shinano Village]] and [[:en:Kami, Nagano|Kamimura]] in {{ill|Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県飯田市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida City, Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. - {{ill|Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|ja|花祭 (霜月神楽)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hana Matsuri (Shimotsuki Kagura)|qq=}}: A traditional folk performance designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in the {{ill|Okumikawa|ja|奥三河|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okumikawa|qq=}} region of [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]].
== References == * Itaya Toru, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|qq=}}* 2 ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3. * Kubota Hiromichi, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten* Vol. 1 ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}, 1998), ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Yamaji Kōzō, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *Nihonshi Daijiten* 3 ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Aoki Shūhei, "Shimotsuki Kagura," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}* 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986), ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. == See Also == * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|ja|冬祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}}
